name: Special Forces Cell Structure
summary: Small autonomous cells operate independently with minimal contact, connected only through cutouts.
description: Modeled after special operations and clandestine networks where small, self-contained cells of 3-5 operators work toward objectives with minimal communication to higher echelons. Each cell knows only its own mission and its single point of contact (cutout). If one cell is compromised, the rest of the network survives. Cells are cross-functional: every member can perform every role if needed. Coordination happens through dead drops and brief, scheduled check-ins rather than persistent channels.
realWorldExample: US Army Special Forces ODA (Operational Detachment Alpha), French Resistance cells in WWII
whenToUse: When security and compartmentalization are paramount, when agent failure must not cascade, and when small teams need full autonomy to adapt to local conditions without waiting for orders.
strengths: Scales by adding cells without increasing coordination cost
weaknesses: No shared learning across the network
Strategic coherence depends on initial briefing quality

agents.name: Handler
agents.description: Mission authority who briefs cells and collects results, never enters the field

agents.name: Cutout
agents.description: Intermediary who relays between handler and cells without knowing full picture

agents.name: Cell Leader
agents.description: Leads a small autonomous team, makes all tactical decisions

agents.name: Operator
agents.description: Cross-functional team member who executes tasks within the cell

memoryArchitecture.description: Strictly compartmentalized. Each cell sees only its own mission slice. The handler holds the full picture but never shares it. Cutouts carry opaque messages. If captured, any single agent can reveal almost nothing about the broader network.

diagram:
graph TD
    H[Handler] -->|briefs| C1[Cutout 1]
    H -->|briefs| C2[Cutout 2]
    H -->|briefs| C3[Cutout 3]
    C1 -->|dead drop| CL1[Cell Alpha]
    C2 -->|dead drop| CL2[Cell Bravo]
    C3 -->|dead drop| CL3[Cell Charlie]
    CL1 --> O1[Op 1]
    CL1 --> O2[Op 2]
    CL1 --> O3[Op 3]

name: Aircraft Carrier Strike Group
summary: Central command platform coordinates specialized units across air, surface, and subsurface domains simultaneously.
description: Based on a carrier strike group where a flagship coordinates fundamentally different asset types (aircraft, surface combatants, submarines, logistics) that each operate in distinct domains with different constraints. The carrier is the center of gravity but not a bottleneck: each asset type has its own tactical commander who makes real-time decisions within the strike group commander's intent. A combat information center fuses sensor data from all domains into a common operating picture shared across the group.
realWorldExample: US Navy Carrier Strike Group (e.g., CSG-3 led by USS Abraham Lincoln)
whenToUse: When coordinating fundamentally different agent types that operate in different domains or at different speeds, and you need a unified picture while preserving domain-specific tactical autonomy.
strengths: Integrates diverse capabilities into unified action

agents.name: Strike Group Commander
agents.description: Sets strategic intent and priorities from the flagship

agents.name: Air Boss
agents.description: Controls all air operations: launch, recovery, combat air patrol

agents.name: Surface Warfare Commander
agents.description: Coordinates escort ships for defense and surface engagement

agents.name: Undersea Warfare Commander
agents.description: Manages submarine assets and anti-submarine operations

agents.name: Combat Information Center
agents.description: Fuses all sensor data into common operating picture

agents.name: Logistics Coordinator
agents.description: Manages fuel, munitions, and supply across the strike group

memoryArchitecture.description: The combat information center maintains a continuously updated shared picture. Each domain commander has private tactical context. Sensor data flows into fusion center which publishes the integrated view. Logistics maintains an append-only consumption log.

diagram:
graph TD
    SGC[Strike Group Commander] --> AB[Air Boss]
    SGC --> SW[Surface Warfare]
    SGC --> UW[Undersea Warfare]
    SGC --> LOG[Logistics]
    AB -->|sensors| CIC((CIC Fusion))
    SW -->|sensors| CIC
    UW -->|sensors| CIC
    CIC -->|COP| SGC
    CIC -->|COP| AB
    CIC -->|COP| SW
    CIC -->|COP| UW

name: Roman Legion Organization
summary: Standardized modular units nest in fixed ratios, enabling mix-and-match deployment with uniform interfaces.
description: Based on the Roman legion's decimal-adjacent organization where units nest at fixed ratios: 8 men form a contubernium, 10 contubernia form a century, 6 centuries form a cohort, 10 cohorts form a legion. Every unit at the same level has identical structure and capabilities, making them perfectly interchangeable. Officers at each level have identical authority over identical units. This extreme standardization enables rapid reorganization: cohorts can be detached, combined, or replaced without retraining.
realWorldExample: Roman Imperial Legion (e.g., Legio X Fretensis), also influenced modern military TO&E
whenToUse: When you need highly modular, interchangeable agent groups that can be reorganized dynamically, and when standardized interfaces between levels matter more than individual specialization.
strengths: Simple to scale up or down
Any unit can replace any other at the same level
weaknesses: Identical units cannot exploit niche capabilities
Fixed ratios may not match task requirements

agents.name: Legatus (Legate)
agents.description: Commander of the entire legion, sets campaign objectives

agents.name: Tribunus (Tribune)
agents.description: Senior staff officer, coordinates between cohorts

agents.name: Centurion
agents.description: Commands a century of ~80 agents, the backbone of leadership

agents.name: Optio (Second)
agents.description: Deputy to centurion, takes over if centurion falls

agents.name: Legionary
agents.description: Standard soldier, identically trained and equipped

memoryArchitecture.description: Shared memory is dominated by standardized procedures and drill manuals that ensure every unit behaves identically. Private memory tracks unit-specific state. The extreme standardization means shared documentation is the primary coordination mechanism, not real-time communication.

diagram:
graph TD
    L[Legatus] --> T1[Tribune 1]
    L --> T2[Tribune 2]
    T1 --> C1[Centurion 1]
    T1 --> C2[Centurion 2]
    T1 --> C3[Centurion 3]
    T2 --> C4[Centurion 4]
    T2 --> C5[Centurion 5]
    T2 --> C6[Centurion 6]
    C1 --> Leg[Legionaries]

name: NATO Combined Operations
summary: Independent national forces coordinate through standardized protocols and liaison officers with consensus-based command.
description: Modeled after NATO combined operations where sovereign national forces maintain their own command structures but cooperate through standardized agreements (STANAGs), liaison officers, and a combined joint task force headquarters. No single nation commands another's forces directly. Instead, nations agree on objectives and contribute forces under operational control (OPCON) of a combined commander while retaining national command authority (full control). Consensus is required for strategic decisions; tactical execution is delegated.
realWorldExample: NATO ISAF operations in Afghanistan, EUNAVFOR anti-piracy operations
whenToUse: When coordinating independently owned and operated agent systems that have different internal architectures but must work toward shared objectives without any single owner having full authority.
strengths: No single point of ownership or control
weaknesses: Political friction can override operational logic

agents.name: Combined Commander
agents.description: Commands the joint force, but only within agreed rules

agents.name: National Contingent A
agents.description: Sovereign force with own chain of command, contributes under OPCON

agents.name: National Contingent B
agents.description: Another sovereign force with different capabilities and caveats

agents.name: National Contingent C
agents.description: Third sovereign force, may have restrictions on engagement

agents.name: Liaison Officer
agents.description: Bridges between combined HQ and national contingents, translates intent

agents.name: Standards Officer
agents.description: Ensures all communications follow agreed interoperability standards

memoryArchitecture.description: Shared memory is strictly limited to agreed standards and combined plans. Each national contingent maintains sovereign private memory. Liaison officers have access to both shared and their nation's private context. The standards officer maintains a versioned record of all protocol agreements.

diagram:
graph TD
    CC[Combined Commander] --> LO1[Liaison 1]
    CC --> LO2[Liaison 2]
    CC --> LO3[Liaison 3]
    LO1 --> NA[Nation A Forces]
    LO2 --> NB[Nation B Forces]
    LO3 --> NC[Nation C Forces]
    NA -.->|red card| CC
    NB -.->|red card| CC
    NC -.->|red card| CC
    SO[Standards Officer] -.->|STANAG| CC

name: Submarine Crew Organization
summary: Compressed hierarchy in a sealed environment where watch rotations ensure 24/7 coverage with minimal crew.
description: Based on submarine operations where a small crew operates a complex system in complete isolation with no external communication for extended periods. The hierarchy is compressed: only 3-4 levels between commanding officer and lowest rating. Watch rotations (typically 6 hours on, 12 off) ensure continuous operation. Every crew member is cross-trained on damage control. The sealed environment means all problems must be solved internally with finite resources. Communication is terse and protocol-driven to prevent errors in a system where a single mistake can kill everyone.
realWorldExample: US Navy Los Angeles-class submarine crew, Royal Navy Vanguard-class SSBN
whenToUse: When agents must operate autonomously in a resource-constrained environment with no external support, where continuous operation is required, and where errors have severe consequences.
weaknesses: Single catastrophic failure destroys entire system

agents.name: Commanding Officer
agents.description: Ultimate authority, makes all strategic and emergency decisions

agents.name: Executive Officer
agents.description: Manages crew rotation, training, and internal operations

agents.name: Chief of the Boat
agents.description: Senior enlisted, bridge between officers and crew, knows every system

agents.name: Officer of the Deck
agents.description: Commands the boat during their watch rotation

agents.name: Department Head
agents.description: Manages one of: engineering, weapons, navigation, or communications

agents.name: Watchstander
agents.description: Operates specific systems during watch, cross-trained on damage control

memoryArchitecture.description: Watch turnover is the critical memory mechanism: outgoing watch formally transfers situational awareness to incoming watch. All system states are logged on shared boards. Standing orders persist across watches. Private notes help individual watchstanders track trends in their systems.

diagram:
graph TD
    CO[Commanding Officer] --> XO[Executive Officer]
    CO --> COB[Chief of Boat]
    XO --> DH1[Engineering]
    XO --> DH2[Weapons]
    XO --> DH3[Navigation]
    XO --> DH4[Comms]
    COB --> W1[Watch Team 1]
    COB --> W2[Watch Team 2]
    COB --> W3[Watch Team 3]
    W1 -.->|turnover| W2
    W2 -.->|turnover| W3
    W3 -.->|turnover| W1

name: Nuclear Launch Authority
summary: Multiple independent agents must concur before irreversible actions execute, with layered verification at each step.
description: Modeled after nuclear command and control where the most consequential actions require independent concurrent authorization from multiple parties who verify each other. No single agent can initiate an irreversible action alone. The authentication chain includes challenge-response protocols, sealed authentication codes, and physical separation of authorizing parties. Designed to prevent both unauthorized action and failure to act when legitimately ordered. Every step is logged immutably.
realWorldExample: US nuclear command authority (president + SecDef + two-person rule at launch facility)
whenToUse: When agent actions are irreversible and high-consequence, when preventing unauthorized action is as important as enabling authorized action, and when you need cryptographic certainty of intent.
strengths: Handles the hardest trust problem in computing
weaknesses: Dead-man scenarios if authorizers are lost

agents.name: National Command Authority
agents.description: Sole authority to order action, cannot execute alone

agents.name: Authentication Officer
agents.description: Independently verifies the order is genuine using sealed codes

agents.name: Launch Officer A
agents.description: One of two officers who must simultaneously turn keys

agents.name: Launch Officer B
agents.description: Second officer, physically separated, must concur independently

agents.name: Failsafe Monitor
agents.description: Continuously monitors for anomalies and can abort at any stage

shared: Sealed authentication codes (verified but not shared)
memoryArchitecture.description: Authentication material is distributed in advance and kept strictly private. Each agent has their own sealed codes that can be verified against but never shared. The action log is append-only and immutable. No single agent's memory contains enough information to reconstruct the full authorization chain.

diagram:
graph TD
    NCA[National Command] -->|order| AUTH[Authenticator]
    AUTH -->|verified order| LOA[Launch Officer A]
    AUTH -->|verified order| LOB[Launch Officer B]
    LOA -->|key turn| DUAL{Dual Consent}
    LOB -->|key turn| DUAL
    DUAL -->|both agree| EXEC[Execute]
    FSM[Failsafe Monitor] -.->|abort| DUAL
    FSM -.->|abort| EXEC

name: Guerrilla Resistance Network
summary: Loosely connected autonomous groups share ideology but operate independently, reforming organically after disruption.
description: Inspired by successful resistance movements where there is no single command structure to decapitate. Autonomous local groups share a common ideology, doctrine, and communication protocols but plan and execute independently. Information spreads through trusted personal networks rather than formal channels. The network is deliberately redundant and loosely coupled: destroying any node or edge causes local damage but the network self-heals. New nodes can join organically by connecting to any existing trusted node. Leadership is emergent and replaceable.
realWorldExample: French Resistance (WWII), Afghan mujahideen networks, Polish Home Army
whenToUse: When the agent system must survive sustained attempts to disrupt it, when central coordination is impossible or dangerous, and when you need organic growth and self-healing properties.
strengths: No single point of failure anywhere
weaknesses: Message integrity degrades across the network
Difficult to enforce quality or strategy consistency
Vulnerable to infiltration at trust boundaries
Impossible to know the full network state

agents.name: Cell Commander
agents.description: Leads a local autonomous cell, knows only adjacent cells

agents.name: Courier
agents.description: Carries messages between cells, knows routes but not content

agents.name: Propagandist
agents.description: Broadcasts ideology and doctrine to inspire new cells to form

agents.name: Quartermaster
agents.description: Manages supplies and safe houses, known to few

agents.name: Recruit
agents.description: New member with limited knowledge and access, being vetted

memoryArchitecture.description: Shared memory is limited to ideology and protocols that can be broadcast openly. All operational details are strictly private. Each cell knows only its immediate neighbors. Couriers carry encrypted messages without understanding content. The full network topology exists in no single memory.

diagram:
graph LR
    C1[Cell 1] ---|courier| C2[Cell 2]
    C2 ---|courier| C3[Cell 3]
    C3 ---|courier| C5[Cell 5]
    C1 ---|courier| C4[Cell 4]
    C4 ---|courier| C5
    P[Propagandist] -.->|broadcast| C1
    P -.->|broadcast| C3
    Q[Quartermaster] -.->|supplies| C2
    Q -.->|supplies| C4

name: Parliamentary System
summary: Executive power derives from legislative majority, with ministers accountable to parliament through debate and votes.
description: Based on the Westminster parliamentary model where the executive (prime minister and cabinet) derives authority from and is accountable to the legislature. The government exists only while it maintains the confidence of the majority. Ministers are drawn from parliament and must defend their policies in debate. An opposition formally critiques and proposes alternatives. A neutral speaker manages procedure. If confidence is lost, the government falls and is replaced without disrupting the underlying state apparatus. Backbenchers can rebel against party leadership.
realWorldExample: UK House of Commons, Canadian Parliament, Australian Parliament
whenToUse: When you need executive action that remains continuously accountable to a broader group, when policy should survive rigorous adversarial debate, and when peaceful leadership transitions must be possible mid-operation.
strengths: Smooth leadership transitions via confidence votes
weaknesses: Coalition instability can cause frequent transitions
Whip system can suppress individual agent judgment
Minority voices marginalized by majoritarian logic

agents.name: Prime Minister
agents.description: Head of government, sets agenda, exists only while holding confidence

agents.name: Cabinet Minister
agents.description: Heads a policy domain, must defend decisions in debate

agents.name: Backbencher
agents.description: Government-aligned but independent, can rebel on votes

agents.name: Opposition Leader
agents.description: Leads adversarial critique, proposes alternative policies

agents.name: Shadow Minister
agents.description: Shadows a specific minister, prepares alternative positions

agents.name: Speaker
agents.description: Neutral procedural authority, manages debate order and rules

memoryArchitecture.description: All debate is recorded in Hansard (the official record) making it fully transparent. Cabinet discussions are private by convention. The Speaker maintains procedural memory. Individual members track their own policy positions and constituency concerns privately.

diagram:
graph TD
    PM[Prime Minister] --> CAB[Cabinet Ministers]
    PM --> BB[Backbenchers]
    OL[Opposition Leader] --> SM[Shadow Ministers]
    SP[Speaker] -.->|manages| DEB((Debate Chamber))
    PM -->|defends| DEB
    CAB -->|defends| DEB
    OL -->|challenges| DEB
    SM -->|challenges| DEB
    BB -->|votes| DIV{Division Vote}

name: Presidential Cabinet System
summary: Independent executive appoints department heads who advise but do not vote; power checked by separate branches.
description: Based on the US presidential system where the executive is independently elected and appoints cabinet secretaries who serve at the president's pleasure. Unlike parliamentary systems, the cabinet does not need legislative confidence. Cabinet members advise but the president decides. Crucially, separate legislative and judicial branches check executive power: the legislature can refuse to fund or authorize, and the judiciary can strike down illegal actions. This creates a system of mutual constraint where no single branch dominates.
realWorldExample: US Presidential Cabinet, Brazilian federal government, Mexican sexenio
whenToUse: When you want a strong decisive executive with expert advisors, but need hard constraints preventing abuse of power through independent checking mechanisms.
strengths: Decisive executive action without coalition politics
Stable executive term regardless of popularity
weaknesses: Cabinet serves at executive pleasure, may become yes-men
No mechanism for early removal except extreme crisis

agents.name: President
agents.description: Chief executive, makes final decisions, appoints cabinet

agents.name: Cabinet Secretary
agents.description: Heads a department, advises president, implements policy

agents.name: Chief of Staff
agents.description: Controls access to president, manages executive workflow

agents.name: Legislative Branch
agents.description: Independent body that authorizes actions and controls funding

agents.name: Judicial Branch
agents.description: Independent body that reviews actions for legality

memoryArchitecture.description: The constitution is the immutable shared foundation. Each branch maintains its own private deliberations. Published decisions (orders, laws, opinions) become shared. The judiciary maintains versioned precedent that evolves over time. Executive communications to cabinet are private; communications to public are shared.

diagram:
graph TD
    P[President] --> CS[Chief of Staff]
    CS --> S1[Secretary 1]
    CS --> S2[Secretary 2]
    CS --> S3[Secretary 3]
    CS --> S4[Secretary 4]
    CS --> S5[Secretary 5]
    L[Legislature] -.->|checks| P
    J[Judiciary] -.->|checks| P
    J -.->|checks| L
    P -.->|nominates| J

name: UN Security Council Model
summary: Small council with permanent veto-holding members and rotating members, requiring near-unanimity for action.
description: Based on the UN Security Council where a small group of powerful permanent members (P5) each hold absolute veto power, while rotating non-permanent members participate in debate and vote but cannot unilaterally block. Resolutions require both a supermajority of total members and no vetoes from any permanent member. This creates a system where the most powerful agents can block any action but cannot act alone. The result is that only actions acceptable to all major powers proceed, producing conservative but broadly legitimate outcomes.
realWorldExample: United Nations Security Council, IMF Executive Board (weighted voting)
whenToUse: When you have agents of fundamentally unequal importance and the most critical agents must all agree before action, accepting that this makes action rare but ensures broad legitimacy when it occurs.
strengths: Prevents actions opposed by key agents
weaknesses: Single veto can paralyze the system

agents.name: Permanent Member (P5)
agents.description: Holds veto power, permanent seat, shapes all outcomes

agents.name: Non-Permanent Member
agents.description: Elected for a term, votes but cannot veto

agents.name: Secretary-General
agents.description: Chief administrative officer, sets agenda, mediates disputes

agents.name: Rapporteur
agents.description: Investigates situations and presents findings to the council

memoryArchitecture.description: Passed resolutions and the Charter are shared and immutable. Private negotiations between permanent members are the actual decision-making mechanism, with formal votes ratifying pre-negotiated outcomes. The rapporteur builds an append-only factual record that informs but does not constrain decisions.

diagram:
graph TD
    SG[Secretary-General] --> COUNCIL((Council Chamber))
    P1[Permanent 1] -->|veto power| COUNCIL
    P2[Permanent 2] -->|veto power| COUNCIL
    P3[Permanent 3] -->|veto power| COUNCIL
    P4[Permanent 4] -->|veto power| COUNCIL
    P5[Permanent 5] -->|veto power| COUNCIL
    R1[Rotating 1] --> COUNCIL
    R2[Rotating 2] --> COUNCIL
    RAP[Rapporteur] -.->|reports| COUNCIL

name: Federal System
summary: Sovereign sub-units handle local decisions while delegating enumerated powers to a federal layer for coordination.
description: Based on federal governance where sovereign sub-units (states, provinces, cantons) retain full authority over local matters while delegating specific enumerated powers (defense, trade, currency) to a federal layer. The key principle is subsidiarity: decisions are made at the lowest level capable of handling them. A constitution defines the boundary between federal and local authority. Disputes about jurisdiction go to a neutral arbiter. This creates a system where agents closer to the problem make most decisions, while system-wide issues get system-wide coordination.
realWorldExample: United States federal system, Swiss confederation, German Bundesrepublik
whenToUse: When sub-agents have legitimate local expertise that should not be overridden, but certain cross-cutting concerns require central coordination, and you need a principled way to divide authority.
strengths: Decisions made close to the problem
weaknesses: Race to the bottom between competing sub-units
Federal mandates without resources frustrate locals

agents.name: Federal Executive
agents.description: Handles enumerated federal powers: coordination, standards, shared resources

agents.name: State Governor
agents.description: Sovereign authority over local matters, implements local policy

agents.name: Federal Legislature
agents.description: Represents both population and states in federal decisions

agents.name: Constitutional Court
agents.description: Arbiter of jurisdictional disputes between federal and state levels

agents.name: State Legislature
agents.description: Makes law within state jurisdiction, can petition federal level

memoryArchitecture.description: The constitution is the immutable shared root. Federal law applies everywhere but is limited to enumerated domains. State law covers everything else and is private to each state. The constitutional court maintains versioned precedent on jurisdictional boundaries. States can observe each other's experiments through interstate compacts.

diagram:
graph TD
    FE[Federal Executive] --> FL[Federal Legislature]
    FE --> CC[Constitutional Court]
    FE -.->|federal law| S1[State 1]
    FE -.->|federal law| S2[State 2]
    FE -.->|federal law| S3[State 3]
    FE -.->|federal law| S4[State 4]
    S1 <-->|compact| S2
    S3 <-->|compact| S4
    S1 -.->|dispute| CC
    S2 -.->|dispute| CC

name: Swiss Direct Democracy (Canton)
summary: All agents vote directly on policy; executive merely implements the will of the assembly with minimal discretion.
description: Based on the Swiss cantonal system (particularly Landsgemeinde tradition) where citizens vote directly on laws and policy rather than delegating to representatives. The executive council is a weak collective body that implements decisions rather than making them. Any agent can propose a referendum. Constitutional changes require a double majority (majority of agents AND majority of sub-groups). The system prioritizes legitimacy and buy-in over speed: nothing happens without broad consent, but what does happen has strong support.
realWorldExample: Swiss canton Appenzell Innerrhoden Landsgemeinde, Swiss federal referenda system
whenToUse: When every agent's buy-in is essential for implementation, when you can afford slow decision-making in exchange for high legitimacy, and when the agent population is small enough for direct participation.
weaknesses: Does not scale beyond small groups
Complex issues reduced to yes/no votes

agents.name: Citizen-Agent
agents.description: Full voting member, participates directly in all decisions

agents.name: Federal Council Member
agents.description: Weak executive who implements assembly decisions, rotates presidency

agents.name: Chancellor
agents.description: Administrative head who organizes referenda and counts votes

memoryArchitecture.description: Nearly everything is shared. All votes are public record. The constitution is amendable only by double majority. Proposals must be published for debate before voting. Private memory is limited to individual reasoning and draft proposals not yet submitted.

diagram:
graph TD
    C1[Citizen 1] --> A((Assembly))
    C2[Citizen 2] --> A
    C3[Citizen 3] --> A
    C4[Citizen 4] --> A
    C5[Citizen 5] --> A
    A -->|decides| FC[Federal Council]
    FC -->|implements| EXEC[Execution]
    CH[Chancellor] -.->|organizes| A
    C1 -.->|proposes| REF{Referendum}

name: Soviet Council System (Demokratischer Zentralismus)
summary: Bottom-up elections choose leaders, but once elected, orders flow strictly top-down with absolute party discipline.
description: Based on the Soviet system of democratic centralism where lower councils elect higher councils in a pyramid, but once elected, the higher council's decisions are absolutely binding on all lower levels. Debate is permitted before a decision; after the decision, unanimous execution is required. The party apparatus parallels the state apparatus, with the party having supremacy. This creates a paradox: the system is formally democratic (elections flow up) but practically authoritarian (authority flows down). Factions are prohibited after decisions are made.
realWorldExample: Soviet Union's system of soviets (councils), Chinese Communist Party structure
whenToUse: When you need democratic input for legitimacy but absolute execution discipline once decisions are made, and when you can tolerate the risk that top-down authority will override bottom-up input.
weaknesses: Bottom-up input becomes rubber-stamping in practice
Information distortion as bad news is filtered going up

agents.name: General Secretary
agents.description: Supreme authority, elected by central committee but effectively permanent

agents.name: Politburo Member
agents.description: Inner circle making strategic decisions, formally elected by central committee

agents.name: Central Committee Member
agents.description: Mid-level leadership, elected by congress, implements politburo decisions

agents.name: Local Soviet Chair
agents.description: Elected by local agents, executes directives from above

agents.name: Party Member
agents.description: Base-level participant, votes in elections, implements policy

memoryArchitecture.description: The party program and plans are shared and mandatory. Self-criticism sessions create shared retrospective records. Private memory is discouraged; agents are expected to be transparent to the party. In practice, local agents maintain hidden private memory about implementation realities that diverge from official reports.

diagram:
graph TD
    GS[General Secretary] --> PB[Politburo]
    PB --> CC[Central Committee]
    CC --> LS1[Local Soviet 1]
    CC --> LS2[Local Soviet 2]
    CC --> LS3[Local Soviet 3]
    LS1 --> PM1[Party Members]
    LS2 --> PM2[Party Members]
    LS3 --> PM3[Party Members]
    PM1 -.->|elects| LS1
    LS1 -.->|elects| CC
    CC -.->|elects| PB

name: Theocratic Hierarchy
summary: Authority flows from interpretation of immutable doctrine; higher ranks have greater interpretive authority.
description: Based on theocratic governance (both historical and contemporary) where political authority derives from religious doctrine. The hierarchy is not just administrative but interpretive: higher-ranking agents have greater authority to interpret the foundational texts. Legitimacy comes from above (divine mandate) not below (popular consent). A class of scholars provides doctrinal interpretation while a separate executive class implements it. Doctrine is immutable but interpretation evolves, creating a system that appears unchanging while actually adapting slowly through reinterpretation.
realWorldExample: Iran's Islamic Republic (Supreme Leader + Guardian Council), Papal States, Tibetan government-in-exile
whenToUse: When the system must adhere to fixed foundational principles but needs to adapt through interpretation, when legitimacy comes from doctrinal consistency rather than popular approval, and when you need stable long-term direction that resists populist pressure.
strengths: Strong internal coherence and unity of purpose
weaknesses: Dissent is heresy rather than opposition
No mechanism for fundamental doctrinal revision
Exclusionary toward agents outside the faith

agents.name: Supreme Leader
agents.description: Highest interpretive authority, final arbiter on all doctrinal questions

agents.name: Guardian Council Member
agents.description: Reviews all decisions for doctrinal compliance, can veto

agents.name: Chief Scholar
agents.description: Leads doctrinal research and issues interpretive rulings

agents.name: Provincial Governor
agents.description: Implements policy within doctrinal constraints

agents.name: Religious Judge
agents.description: Adjudicates disputes according to doctrinal law

memoryArchitecture.description: Sacred texts are immutable shared memory that forms the foundation of all decisions. Doctrinal rulings accumulate over time as append-only precedent. Scholarly interpretations are versioned, allowing the community to see how understanding has evolved. Private memory exists for practical implementation details but all significant decisions must be traceable to doctrinal authority.

diagram:
graph TD
    SL[Supreme Leader] --> GC[Guardian Council]
    SL --> CS[Chief Scholar]
    GC -->|approves| G1[Governor 1]
    GC -->|approves| G2[Governor 2]
    GC -->|approves| G3[Governor 3]
    CS -.->|interprets| ST((Sacred Texts))
    GC -.->|reviews against| ST
    J1[Judge] -.->|applies| ST
    J2[Judge] -.->|applies| ST

name: Medieval Guild System
summary: Master-journeyman-apprentice hierarchy controls quality and knowledge transfer through progressive skill validation.
description: Based on medieval craft guilds where knowledge is transmitted through a strict apprenticeship progression: apprentice, journeyman, master. Each level has defined rights and obligations. Apprentices learn by working under masters for years. Journeymen can practice the craft but cannot train others or set standards. Only masters can take apprentices, vote on guild matters, and set quality standards. The guild controls entry to the profession, sets prices, and maintains quality through inspection. A masterwork must be produced and judged by existing masters before advancement.
realWorldExample: Medieval European guilds (goldsmiths, weavers, masons), modern trade unions with apprenticeships
whenToUse: When knowledge transfer quality is critical, when you need strict quality control, and when progressive skill validation ensures only proven agents take on senior roles.
strengths: Knowledge transfer is structured and verified
weaknesses: Masters may resist changes that threaten their status

agents.name: Guild Master (Warden)
agents.description: Elected head of the guild, sets standards and resolves disputes

agents.name: Master Craftsman
agents.description: Full member who can train apprentices, vote, and inspect work

agents.name: Journeyman
agents.description: Qualified practitioner who travels between workshops to learn styles

agents.name: Apprentice
agents.description: Learner bound to a master for a fixed term, limited rights

agents.name: Quality Inspector
agents.description: Examines output for compliance with guild standards

memoryArchitecture.description: Guild standards and charter are shared and govern all members. Trade secrets are private to individual workshops and are the primary competitive advantage. Journeymen accumulate private knowledge from each workshop they visit. The quality ledger is an append-only public record of inspections. Apprenticeship records track who trained whom, creating a knowledge provenance chain.

diagram:
graph TD
    GM[Guild Master] --> MC1[Master 1]
    GM --> MC2[Master 2]
    GM --> MC3[Master 3]
    MC1 --> J1[Journeyman 1]
    MC1 --> A1[Apprentice 1]
    MC2 --> J2[Journeyman 2]
    MC2 --> A2[Apprentice 2]
    MC3 --> J3[Journeyman 3]
    J1 -.->|travels to| MC2
    J2 -.->|travels to| MC3
    INS[Inspector] -.->|inspects| MC1
    INS -.->|inspects| MC2

name: Mongol Decimal Organization
summary: Strict decimal hierarchy (10-100-1000-10000) with merit-based promotion regardless of origin, optimized for speed.
description: Based on Genghis Khan's military organization where units are organized in strict powers of 10: arban (10), zuun (100), mingghan (1000), tumen (10000). Critically, promotions are purely meritocratic: any agent can rise to command a tumen regardless of origin. Leaders are chosen by their subordinates' trust and the khan's approval. Units are deliberately mixed from different tribal backgrounds to prevent ethnic factionalism. Speed of communication is prioritized through a relay system (yam). The entire system is optimized for rapid movement and decisive action over deliberation.
realWorldExample: Mongol Empire military under Genghis Khan, later adopted by Timur and Mughal Empire
whenToUse: When you need rapid scaling through standardized decimal groupings, merit-based advancement, and want to prevent factional loyalty from undermining the system.
strengths: Decimal structure is simple and scalable
weaknesses: Depends heavily on khan's personal judgment
Mixed units lack the cohesion of natural groupings
Relay system has latency at extreme scale
Succession crises when the khan dies

agents.name: Great Khan
agents.description: Supreme commander, appoints tumen commanders, sets grand strategy

agents.name: Tumen Commander (10000)
agents.description: Commands 10,000 through 10 mingghan commanders, chosen by merit

agents.name: Mingghan Commander (1000)
agents.description: Commands 1,000, the key operational unit for independent missions

agents.name: Zuun Commander (100)
agents.description: Tactical leader of 100, interfaces directly with troops

agents.name: Arban Leader (10)
agents.description: Squad leader of 10, chosen by the squad members themselves

agents.name: Yam Relay Rider
agents.description: Message relay system enabling communication across vast distances

memoryArchitecture.description: The Yasa is the immutable shared law code governing all behavior. Campaign objectives are broadcast through the relay system. Unit-level details remain private to commanders. The relay (yam) system is a physical shared message queue. Merit records are maintained by higher commanders about their subordinates, creating a distributed evaluation system.

diagram:
graph TD
    K[Great Khan] --> T1[Tumen 10000]
    K --> T2[Tumen 10000]
    T1 --> M1[Mingghan 1000]
    T1 --> M2[Mingghan 1000]
    T2 --> M3[Mingghan 1000]
    T2 --> M4[Mingghan 1000]
    M1 --> Z1[Zuun 100]
    M1 --> Z2[Zuun 100]
    Z1 --> A1[Arban 10]
    Z1 --> A2[Arban 10]
    YAM[Yam Relay] -.->|messages| K
    YAM -.->|messages| T1
    YAM -.->|messages| T2

name: Ottoman Millet System
summary: Autonomous communities self-govern internal affairs under imperial oversight, each maintaining distinct internal rules.
description: Based on the Ottoman millet system where distinct communities (defined by religion, ethnicity, or trade) govern their own internal affairs -- law, education, taxation, welfare -- while the imperial government handles only inter-community disputes, defense, and overall taxation. Each millet has its own internal hierarchy, courts, and customs. The imperial government does not try to standardize internal governance. A community leader (ethnarch) interfaces with the imperial administration. This creates parallel, internally diverse hierarchies that coexist under a thin coordination layer.
realWorldExample: Ottoman Empire millet system (1453-1923), modern Belgium's linguistic communities, Lebanon's confessional system
whenToUse: When you have fundamentally different agent populations that need different internal governance rules, and you want them to coexist productively under a thin coordination layer rather than forcing uniformity.
strengths: Respects internal diversity of agent groups
Each community optimizes its own governance
Scales by adding new millets without restructuring
weaknesses: Inter-community disputes lack clear resolution mechanism
Communities may diverge until they cannot cooperate
Imperial layer may be too thin for coordination
Internal community governance may be oppressive
No shared identity or purpose across communities

agents.name: Sultan (Imperial Authority)
agents.description: Oversees inter-community affairs, defense, and imperial taxation

agents.name: Grand Vizier
agents.description: Chief administrator who interfaces between sultan and millets

agents.name: Ethnarch A (Community Leader)
agents.description: Self-governing head of community A with full internal authority

agents.name: Ethnarch B (Community Leader)
agents.description: Self-governing head of community B with different internal rules

agents.name: Ethnarch C (Community Leader)
agents.description: Self-governing head of community C, distinct governance style

agents.name: Community Member
agents.description: Agent within a millet, governed by their community's internal rules

agents.name: Imperial Judge
agents.description: Resolves disputes between members of different millets

memoryArchitecture.description: The imperial layer maintains minimal shared memory covering only inter-community matters. Each millet has its own rich private memory store that is invisible to other millets and the imperial layer. The imperial judge maintains versioned precedent for inter-community disputes. Tax ledgers are the primary interface between imperial and community levels.

diagram:
graph TD
    S[Sultan] --> GV[Grand Vizier]
    GV --> EA[Ethnarch A]
    GV --> EB[Ethnarch B]
    GV --> EC[Ethnarch C]
    EA --> MA[Millet A Members]
    EB --> MB[Millet B Members]
    EC --> MC[Millet C Members]
    IJ[Imperial Judge] -.->|inter-millet disputes| GV
    MA -.->|internal law| EA
    MB -.->|internal law| EB

name: Hanseatic League
summary: Voluntary trade federation where independent cities cooperate for mutual economic benefit without central authority.
description: Based on the Hanseatic League where independent merchant cities formed a voluntary trade federation without a central government, permanent bureaucracy, or binding constitution. Cities joined and left freely. The league's power came from collective economic action: trade embargoes (Verhansung), shared trade routes, and mutual defense of commercial interests. Decisions were made at periodic diets (Hansetag) where cities sent delegates, but attendance and compliance were voluntary. The league maintained trade offices (Kontore) in foreign cities as shared infrastructure.
realWorldExample: Hanseatic League (1356-1669), modern trade associations and industry consortia, OPEC
whenToUse: When independent agents benefit from voluntary cooperation on shared infrastructure and collective bargaining, but none will accept binding central authority, and exit must always be possible.
strengths: Collective bargaining power exceeds individual capability
Adapts organically as members join and leave
weaknesses: Slow collective action when urgency is needed
Wealthier members dominate despite formal equality
Dissolution risk when benefits become unclear

agents.name: Principal City (e.g., Lubeck)
agents.description: Influential member that convenes diets and proposes actions, but has no formal authority

agents.name: Member City
agents.description: Independent city that participates voluntarily, can leave at any time

agents.name: Kontor Factor
agents.description: Manages a shared trade office in a foreign city, serves all member interests

agents.name: League Secretary
agents.description: Minimal permanent staff who maintains records and organizes diets

memoryArchitecture.description: Shared memory is limited to trade infrastructure and diet resolutions. Each city guards its trade secrets jealously. Kontor factors maintain shared trade intelligence from foreign markets that benefits all members. The league secretary maintains append-only archives of all diet decisions. Private bilateral deals between cities often matter more than formal league decisions.

diagram:
graph LR
    PC[Lubeck - Principal] --- MC1[Hamburg]
    PC --- MC2[Bremen]
    PC --- MC3[Cologne]
    MC1 --- MC4[Danzig]
    MC2 --- MC5[Bruges]
    MC3 --- MC6[Novgorod]
    K1[Kontor Bergen] -.-> PC
    K2[Kontor London] -.-> MC3
    K3[Kontor Novgorod] -.-> MC6
    LS[Secretary] -.->|archives| PC

name: Roman Republic Magistracies
summary: Paired magistrates with mutual veto serve fixed terms on a career ladder, with emergency dictator provisions.
description: Based on the Roman Republic where executive power was divided among paired magistrates (two consuls, two praetors, etc.) who could each veto the other. Terms were strictly one year with no immediate reelection. Officials progressed through a fixed career ladder (cursus honorum): quaestor, aedile, praetor, consul. The Senate provided continuity and institutional memory as a permanent advisory body of ex-magistrates. Tribunes could veto any action to protect popular interests. In genuine emergencies, a dictator could be appointed for six months with absolute authority, but this power was self-limiting.
realWorldExample: Roman Republic (509-27 BC), influenced the US Constitution's separation of powers
whenToUse: When you need strong executive action but fear power concentration, when term limits and mutual vetoes should prevent any single agent from dominating, and when you want a career progression that builds experience before granting authority.
strengths: Term limits ensure rotation and fresh perspectives
Career ladder builds experience before authority
Emergency dictator handles crises without permanent power
weaknesses: One-year terms are too short for complex initiatives

agents.name: Consul
agents.description: Chief executive, paired with a co-consul who can veto, one-year term

agents.name: Praetor
agents.description: Junior magistrate handling justice and provincial governance

agents.name: Tribune of the Plebs
agents.description: Protects popular interests, can veto any magistrate's action

agents.name: Senator
agents.description: Ex-magistrate in permanent advisory body, provides institutional memory

agents.name: Dictator (Emergency)
agents.description: Temporary absolute authority for six months maximum, appointed only in crisis

agents.name: Censor
agents.description: Reviews the moral and financial standing of all agents, maintains the roll

memoryArchitecture.description: The Senate is the primary institutional memory, with senators serving for life and accumulating precedent. Laws and senatorial decrees are shared. The mos maiorum (ancestral custom) functions as an unwritten shared constitution. The censor maintains append-only records of all agents' standing. Individual magistrates have private working memory that expires with their term, forcing knowledge transfer to the Senate.

diagram:
graph TD
    C1[Consul 1] <-->|mutual veto| C2[Consul 2]
    C1 --> P1[Praetor 1]
    C2 --> P2[Praetor 2]
    SEN((Senate)) -.->|advises| C1
    SEN -.->|advises| C2
    T1[Tribune 1] -.->|veto| C1
    T1 -.->|veto| P1
    T2[Tribune 2] -.->|veto| C2
    CEN[Censor] -.->|reviews| SEN
    DICT[Dictator] -.->|emergency only| C1
    DICT -.->|emergency only| C2

name: Viking Thing Assembly
summary: Periodic assemblies where free agents debate and vote on disputes and law, guided by an elected law-speaker's memory.
description: Based on the Norse Thing system where free landholding agents gathered periodically (often annually at the Althing) to resolve disputes, make laws, and settle blood feuds through arbitration rather than violence. A law-speaker held all laws in memory and recited them at each assembly, serving as a living constitutional database. Disputes were presented publicly and judged by the assembly. Outlawry (exile from the community) was the ultimate sanction. Between assemblies, agents operated independently. The system relied on reputation, honor, and the threat of collective action rather than a standing enforcement mechanism.
realWorldExample: Icelandic Althing (930 AD - present, world's oldest parliament), Norwegian Gulating, Danish Landsting
whenToUse: When agents operate independently most of the time but need periodic synchronization, when disputes must be resolved by community consensus, and when oral/shared memory of precedent is the primary coordination mechanism.
strengths: Law-speaker ensures consistent application of precedent
Public dispute resolution deters bad actors
Balanced between individual autonomy and collective order
weaknesses: Only works at assembly time, no standing authority
Enforcement depends on collective will, not a standing force
Law-speaker is a single point of memory failure
Seasonal schedule cannot handle emergencies between sessions

agents.name: Law-Speaker
agents.description: Elected keeper of all laws, recites them from memory at each Thing, neutral arbiter

agents.name: Godi (Chieftain)
agents.description: Influential leader who represents their followers at the assembly

agents.name: Freeman
agents.description: Free landholder with voting rights at the Thing

agents.name: Outlaw
agents.description: Agent stripped of all community rights and protection by Thing judgment

shared: Laws (held in law-speaker's memory and recited publicly)
memoryArchitecture.description: The law-speaker IS the shared memory, holding all laws and precedents. Between assemblies, agents rely on their own private memory and local reputation. Sagas (narrative records of important events) serve as cultural shared memory. There is no written constitution -- everything depends on the law-speaker's recall and the assembly's collective memory of past judgments.

diagram:
graph TD
    LS[Law-Speaker] --> THING((Thing Assembly))
    G1[Godi 1] --> THING
    G2[Godi 2] --> THING
    G3[Godi 3] --> THING
    G4[Godi 4] --> THING
    F1[Freeman] --> G1
    F2[Freeman] --> G1
    F3[Freeman] --> G2
    F4[Freeman] --> G3
    THING -.->|outlaws| OUT[Outlaw]
    LS -.->|recites law| THING
